text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to mobilize the colon, taking down the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon. The sigmoid colon is visibly mobilized as a result of this dissection.

The surgeon gradually mobilizes the colon and rectum by dividing the peritoneum on the left side, taking care to avoid injuring the ureter. A similar dissection is performed on the right side of the patient.

The surgeon performs dissection in the presacral fascia, using a harmonic scalpel to separate the mesorectum, which is taken up with the specimen. The lateral ligaments are carefully dissected and divided with the harmonic scalpel, while a monopolar hook cautery is mentioned as an alternative tool for dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection down into the pelvis, where the inferior mesenteric vein is isolated, clipped with hemolog clips, and divided.

The surgeon applies traction to the colon to visualize the sacral fascia, which is being dissected further caudally. The lateral ligaments are dissected and divided on both sides using surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses a bowel grasper to pull the rectum cranially, facilitating further dissection and mobilization within the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to further divide the lateral ligaments. Dissection proceeds between the bladder and rectum, with careful preservation of the seminal vesicles visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon clips the inferior mesenteric artery using hemolog clips and divides it, with visible pulsations of the artery observed during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the caudal dissection, advancing deeper into the pelvic region.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect and divide the mesocolon in the area designated for colonic transection.
The surgeon uses an endo GIA stapler to transect the colon, completing the division of the colonic tissue.

The surgeon creates a permanent colostomy using the descending colon and removes the specimen through the perineal dissection, completing the abdominoperineal resection. The video also shows multiple liver metastasectomy procedures performed with an open approach.
